# Extracao: transcription_mp4_07-Integrate_Financially

- **Fonte:** transcription_mp4_07-Integrate_Financially.txt
- **Tipo:** TXT

---

Financially integrating into your new company covers things like the monthly financial requirements, the revenue and earnings targets for the organization, compensation targets for your team to get them actively involved with the business, and the different leverage points in the business that you just acquired to create the maximum value possible. To do this, you should establish a new chart of accounts and financial statements. Now that you've just bought this business, you should work with your accountant to break down the financials in a way that gives you the most amount of information to make instantaneous decisions as fast as possible. Here's a tip. Almost all businesses that we manage have horrible financial records, which means all the years that those business owners have been running their business, they have not been able to optimize decision making in order to create maximum value. There is a formula that you can use, it's not overly complicated, to maximize the profitability of your business. Most business owners, they run their business financially based on feel, and then they figured out how they did it tax time. That is not an efficient or effective or responsible way to run a business. What you want to have in your financial integration is you want to have a budget for the year, you want to have a target, you want to forecast sales, all the things that you want to be able to put in place. So if you buy a $5 million business and you want to drive it to $10 million in revenue, you should engineer the financial plan to do that. What things would you need to track to know that you're moving in the right direction to accomplish that goal? What profitability do you achieve when you do $5 million in sales? Set those targets in advance so that you can work the business to the targets. That way you can put things in place with your team members, incentives, projects that they can do to create massive and more value. This is running a responsible business and financially integrating into the business. Not to mention that now that you've taken it on the new business, you've got things like payroll, you may have a capital you need to pay in to keep the business going if they've depleted it based on the kind of financing used to buy it. Financial integration is the thing the faster you can get your hands around this, the better for you and the better for the business. You should set up a brand new chart of accounts, have your account populate your revenue targets, the activities that you need to accomplish in order to achieve your revenue targets, and you should be driving activity every single day to that goal. For example, if I bought a new business that was doing $3 million and I said I wanted to do $5 million, I would reverse engineer the business to a daily number. I would want to know how much revenue I would need to do every single day, how much cash I collect against that revenue, so that I can track my daily sales against target. What this does is it teaches you the algorithm for predicting how your months are going. Imagine if you've been running a business that does a quarter of a million dollars a month. You put some new goals in place to take it to 400,000 a month and every day you're tracking your revenue in and your cash collected, so you're reporting that every single morning to yourself. What would happen to your confidence if you were halfway through the month and you realized you're going to hit your target? Would you back off or would you lean in? The more algorithmically you can view your business, the more intuition you create about the business. If every day all you're looking at is sales for the day, cash collected for the day and you do that every day for three months, four months, six months, nine months, you start to get a feel for how the business is doing. When you're initiating new activities through your sales team or through marketing, you can see the impact because all of a sudden if your algorithm is you're collecting $50,000 a day and you start doing something new and that pops to $70,000 a day and you start doing something newer and it pops to $90,000, you know that you're going in the right direction. But if you're watching your cash sales and it's doing $50,000 a day and you try something new and it drops to $40,000, you try something new again and it drops to $30,000, you need to go back to what you're doing. If you don't create that intuition for your business by paying attention to the granular financial details, money in, money out, revenues generated, cash collected, you just start that algorithm the sooner you get in front of it, the faster you can build a financially healthy business that will pay you dividends for the rest of your life. So that is what financial integration into your business is.
